title: Casual Hello — How's it going?
translation: Hey, how's it going? (said to a male friend)

words.meaning: hey (used when speaking to a male)
hey (used when speaking to a female)

practice.question: When greeting a female friend casually, which word do you use to address her?

title: How are you? (with male/female forms)
translation: How are you? (said to a male)

practice.question: How would you ask a female friend 'How are you?'

title: I'm doing great!
translation: I'm doing great! / I'm in good spirits! (you'll often hear just 'मजेत!' on its own)

practice.question: What does मजेत (majet) mean?

title: Thank you
translation: Thank you so much! (the everyday phrase you'll hear first; many also just say 'थँक यू')

words.meaning: thank you (the borrowed English version, used casually all the time)

practice.question: Which is the most common, everyday way to say 'thank you' in spoken Marathi?

title: Saying 'please' the Marathi way
translation: Could you wait a moment, please?

words.meaning: just, a little (softens a request into 'please')

practice.question: Which little word softens a request into a polite 'please' in Marathi?

title: Yes, No, and 'I don't want it'
translation: Yes, that works! / Sure, that's fine!

practice.question: Which word means 'no — I don't want it'?

title: Introducing yourself
translation: My name is Aarav. (in quick speech people often drop 'आहे' → 'माझं नाव आरव.')

practice.question: How do you say 'my' in Marathi (as in 'my name')?

title: Asking someone's name
translation: What's your name? (informal — in spoken Marathi 'आहे' is usually dropped)

practice.question: Which is the polite / formal way to say 'your'?

title: Where are you from?
translation: Where are you from? (said to a male — literally 'where did you come from?')

practice.question: How would you ask a female 'Where are you from?'

title: Saying goodbye — the Marathi way
translation: Okay, I'll be off. / See you. (said by a male; a woman says 'बरं, येते.') Marathi doesn't really have a word for 'goodbye' — people say 'I'll come [again]' instead.

words.meaning: I'll be off / see you (said by a male; literally 'I come')
I'll be off / see you (said by a female)
see you / let's meet (a friendly 'catch you later')

practice.question: A woman is leaving. Which is the natural thing for her to say?

title: Fillers & conversation words
translation: Oh, totally! / Right?! (these little words are sprinkled through almost every Marathi chat)

words.meaning: hey / oh (to a male, or as a general exclamation)
hey / oh (when speaking to a female)
okay / well... (also used as a soft filler)

practice.question: Which filler would you use to get a female friend's attention?

title: What are you doing?
translation: What are you doing? (right now, to a male — a woman is asked 'काय करतेयस?')

practice.question: How do you ask a female friend 'what are you doing?'

title: Where are you?
translation: Where are you? (the message you send when someone's running late)

words.meaning: I'm coming / on my way (said by a male)
I'm coming / on my way (said by a female)

practice.question: Which word means 'here'?

title: I'm at home
translation: I'm at home. (note: 'घरी' = 'at home', a special form of 'घर')

practice.question: What does घरी (ghari) mean?

title: Food & eating
translation: I'm hungry. (literally 'hunger has struck me' — Marathi expresses hunger as something that happens to you)

words.meaning: to me / I (used in 'I feel...' sentences)

practice.question: How do you say 'I'm hungry' in Marathi?

title: Did you eat?
translation: Did you eat? (to a male — a woman is asked 'जेवलीस का?'). In Marathi homes this is a warm, caring way to check in on someone, almost like 'how are you doing?'

practice.question: How do you ask a female 'did you eat?'

title: Time & today
translation: Not right now, let's meet tomorrow. (handy: Hindi uses 'कल' for both — Marathi splits it into उद्या = tomorrow and काल = yesterday)

practice.question: Which word means 'right now'?

title: Friends & hanging out
translation: I'm heading out with friends. (said by a male; a woman says 'चाललेय')

practice.question: What is the Marathi word for a female friend?

title: What do you like?
translation: I like tea. (in Marathi the verb agrees with the thing you like: चहा is masculine → आवडतो; for a neuter thing you'd say आवडतं)

practice.question: How do you say 'I don't like it'?

title: Making simple plans
translation: Come on, let's go to a movie! (the '-ऊया' ending is the everyday 'let's...' form)

practice.question: Which word means 'let's meet'?

title: What are you doing right now?
translation: Hey, what are you doing right now? (to a female; to a male: 'आत्ता काय करतोयस?')

practice.question: How do you ask a male friend what he's doing right now?

title: Where are you going?
translation: Hey, where are you off to? (to a male; to a female: 'कुठे चाललीयस?' or 'कुठे जातेयस?')

words.meaning: where are you headed (male, right now)

practice.question: How would you ask a female 'where are you going?'

title: I'm busy / I'm free
translation: I'm a bit busy, let's talk later. ('कामात आहे' = literally 'in work' — the everyday way to say busy)

practice.question: Which word means 'free / not busy' (for a male)?

title: What happened?
translation: Hey, what happened? (the go-to question when someone looks off)

practice.question: What does काय झालं? mean?

title: No problem
translation: No problem! / That's totally fine!

words.meaning: let it go / forget about it

practice.question: Which phrase means 'never mind / let it go'?

title: I understand / I don't understand
translation: Oh, I didn't get it. (just say 'समजलं' on its own to mean 'got it!')

practice.question: How do you say 'I didn't understand'?

title: Wait a minute
translation: Hey, hold on a sec! ('जरा थांब' is softer and friendlier than just 'थांब')

practice.question: Which means 'I'm just coming!' said by a female?

title: Really?
translation: Wait, really?! (the reaction you give when you can't believe something)

words.meaning: no way! / you don't say! (said to a male)
no way! / you don't say! (said to a female)

practice.question: Which word means 'really? / seriously?'

title: Sounds good
translation: That's great! Works for me.

practice.question: Which word is a casual way to say 'awesome / cool'?

title: Let's go
translation: Come on, let's get going! (use 'चल' for one friend; 'चला' for a group or to be polite)

words.meaning: come on / let's go (to one person, informal)
come on / let's go (to a group or politely)

practice.question: You're telling a group (or being polite). Which 'let's go' do you use?

title: Things I like
translation: I like listening to songs. (the pattern 'मला [verb]-आयला आवडतं' = 'I like to ___' — plug in any action)

practice.question: How do you say 'I like to read'?

title: Things I don't like
sentence: अरे, मला गर्दी अजिबात आवडत नाही.
transliteration: Are, mala gardi ajibaat aavadat naahi.
translation: Ugh, I don't like crowds at all. ('अजिबात' strengthens a 'no' — 'not at all')

words.meaning: at all (with a negative: 'not at all')

practice.question: Which word means 'at all' (used to strengthen a 'no')?

title: Talking about hobbies
translation: I really love watching movies.

practice.question: Which word means 'to watch'?

title: Happy / sad / tired
translation: I'm so tired today. (said by a male; a woman says 'थकले आहे'. Note: आनंदी and उदास stay the same for both genders, but थकलो/थकले changes)

practice.question: A woman says she's tired. Which is correct?

title: Excited / nervous
translation: Ugh, I'm so nervous / stressed! ('टेन्शन आलंय' is how people really say it; the pattern 'X वाटतंय' = 'feeling X')

practice.question: Which is a very common spoken word for feeling nervous / stressed?

title: Giving opinions
translation: I think this is right. ('मला वाटतं' is the everyday way to open an opinion)

practice.question: How do you say 'I think...' to start an opinion?

title: Agreeing & disagreeing
translation: Right, exactly! (to disagree gently you'd say 'नाही रे' to a male or 'नाही गं' to a female)

practice.question: Which means a casual 'nah / no way' said to a female?

title: Making plans
translation: Shall we meet tomorrow evening? (adding 'का' turns 'let's meet' into 'shall we meet?')

practice.question: Which word means 'in the evening'?

title: Inviting someone
translation: Hey, will you come too? ('येशील का?' works for both genders; 'येणार का?' is an equally common, slightly more casual version)

words.meaning: are you coming? / will you come?

practice.question: How do you invite a friend, 'will you come too?'

title: Talking about your day
translation: Today went great! (literally 'today the day went great'. To say what you did, use 'केलं': 'मी खूप काम केलं' = 'I did a lot of work')

practice.question: What does 'आज दिवस मस्त गेला' mean?

title: Common Marathi reactions
translation: Oh wow! Awesome! (these one-word reactions are the heartbeat of Marathi chat — 'अरे वा' for good news, 'बापरे' for shock, 'अरेरे' for sympathy)

words.meaning: oh my! / yikes! (shock, or a big amount)

practice.question: Which reaction shows sympathy, like 'oh no / aww'?

title: Surprise & disbelief
translation: No way! Really? (what you blurt out when something's hard to believe)

practice.question: Which word means 'no way! / impossible!'?

title: Talking about the weather
translation: Whoa, it's so hot today! (note: rain 'falls' in Marathi — 'पाऊस पडतोय' = it's raining)

practice.question: How do you say 'it's raining'?

title: Work & studies
translation: I've got a lot of work, and studying to do too. ('करायचाय' = 'have to do' — a very handy spoken form)

words.meaning: have to do / need to do

practice.question: Which word means 'a day off / holiday'?

title: Future plans
translation: Tomorrow I'm going to my hometown. ('गावी जाणं' — going to your native place — is a very Maharashtrian thing to say. '-णार आहे' = a planned 'going to'; '-ईन/-एन' = a simple 'I will')

practice.question: Which means 'I will meet'?

title: Past experiences
sentence: मी काल सिनेमा बघायला गेलो होतो.
transliteration: Mi kaal cinema baghaayla gelo hoto.
translation: I'd gone to watch a movie yesterday. (said by a male; a woman says 'गेले होते'. The '-लो/-ले होतो/होते' ending is how you narrate something you did)

practice.question: A woman says 'I had gone'. Which is correct?

title: Phone & texting
translation: Hey, call me later, will you? (that little 'ना' at the end turns an order into a friendly request — very Marathi)

words.meaning: softening particle (makes a request gentle, friendly)

practice.question: Which little word makes 'call me' sound friendly rather than bossy?

title: Phrases people use all the time
translation: What's up these days? (the everyday 'what's going on'. A classic non-answer reply is 'असंच!' — 'nothing much')

words.meaning: nothing much / just the usual (a classic non-answer)

practice.question: Someone asks 'काय चाललंय?' — which casual reply means 'nothing much / just the usual'?

title: Real Marathi small talk
translation: Hey, long time no see! How's it going? (exactly what you say running into a friend or neighbour)

words.meaning: after a long time / long time no see
see you then / catch you later

practice.question: What does 'बऱ्याच दिवसांनी!' mean?

title: Putting it all together
sentence: अरे, बऱ्याच दिवसांनी! कसं काय? — मजेत रे! तू बोल, काय चाललंय? — असंच! चल, नंतर निवांत भेटूया. — बरं, भेटू मग!
transliteration: Are, baryaach divsaanni! Kasa kaay? — Majet re! Tu bol, kaay chaallay? — Asanch! Chal, nantar nivaant bhetuya. — Bara, bhetu mag!
translation: Hey, long time no see! How's it going? — Great! You tell me, what's up? — Nothing much! Come on, let's meet up properly sometime. — Okay, see you then! (a full natural exchange built from everything you've learned. Bonus word: 'निवांत' = relaxed, unhurried — a beautifully Marathi word)

practice.question: In this exchange, what does the reply 'असंच!' mean?
